causal_chain.to: Water molecules gain kinetic energy when heated
causal_chain.mechanism: Atmospheric pressure exerts a force on the surface of the water, hindering the escape of water molecules into the gaseous phase. Heating increases the kinetic energy of the water molecules.
Heat is a form of energy that is transferred to the water molecules, increasing their motion.

When the kinetic energy of the water molecules is sufficient to overcome the intermolecular forces holding them together in the liquid phase and the atmospheric pressure, they transition to the gaseous phase (boiling).
At standard atmospheric pressure (sea level), the temperature at which the vapor pressure of water equals the atmospheric pressure is 100 degrees Celsius.
